docs.title: Welcome to Natrix
docs.text: Natrix is a static analysis tool for Vyper contracts that helps you identify potential issues, style violations, and optimization opportunities in your smart contracts.

docs.title: Why Natrix?
docs.text:
Smart contracts on blockchain platforms manage valuable assets and are immutable once deployed. Static analysis helps detect potential issues before deployment, saving gas costs and preventing security vulnerabilities.

Natrix focuses specifically on Vyper, providing tailored analysis for this smart contract language.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
Natrix performs various checks on your Vyper code, including:

  • Style checks (naming conventions, explicit decorators)
  • Gas optimization suggestions (storage caching, memory expansion)
  • Code quality warnings (unused variables and arguments)
  • Security best practices

For a complete list of rules, see the Rules section.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
Ready to start using Natrix? Check out our Quick Start guide for installation and basic usage instructions.

For advanced configuration options, see the Configuration guide.

docs.title: Acknowledgements
docs.text:
Natrix would not be possible without the support of:

  • Builder ENS Grants - For providing a grant to support the development of Natrix.
  • Charles Cooper - Vyper core developer, for his invaluable help with the AST implementation and technical guidance.
  • Curve Finance - For providing complex smart contract challenges that inspired the creation of this linter.

Thank you to all contributors and supporters who have helped make this project a reality!

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Natrix can be configured using a pyproject.toml file in your project root. All configuration options are placed under the [tool.natrix] section.

docs.title: Basic Configuration
docs.text:
[tool.natrix]
# Files or directories to lint (relative to pyproject.toml location)
files = ["contracts/", "tests/contracts/"]

# Rules to disable globally
disabled_rules = ["NTX1", "NTX2"]

# Rule-specific configurations
[tool.natrix.rule_configs.MemoryExpansion]
max_frame_size = 25000

[tool.natrix.rule_configs.ArgNamingConvention]
pattern = "^_[a-z][a-z0-9_]*$"

docs.title: Files to Lint
docs.text:
Specify which files or directories natrix should analyze:

[tool.natrix]
# Single file
files = ["contract.vy"]

# Multiple files and directories
files = ["contracts/", "tests/", "standalone.vy"]

# Relative paths are resolved relative to pyproject.toml location
files = ["src/contracts/", "../shared/contracts/"]

If no files configuration is provided, natrix will scan the current directory and all subdirectories for .vy files.

docs.title: Disabling Rules
docs.text:
Disable specific rules globally across your project:

[tool.natrix]
# Disable single rule
disabled_rules = ["NTX8"]

# Disable multiple rules
disabled_rules = ["NTX1", "NTX7", "NTX11"]

You can find all available rule codes by running: 

natrix --list-rules

docs.title: Rule-Specific Configuration
docs.text:
Some rules accept configuration parameters to customize their behavior. Only two rules currently have configurable parameters:

Memory Expansion Rule (NTX1) 

[tool.natrix.rule_configs.MemoryExpansion]
max_frame_size = 25000  # Default: 20000

Argument Naming Convention Rule (NTX11) 

[tool.natrix.rule_configs.ArgNamingConvention]
pattern = "^_[a-z][a-z0-9_]*$"  # Default: "^_"

For detailed configuration examples and advanced usage, see the individual rule documentation pages in the Rules section.

docs.title: Example Configurations
docs.text:
DeFi ProtocolLearning ProjectMinimal 
[tool.natrix]
files = ["contracts/core/", "contracts/interfaces/", "tests/unit/"]
disabled_rules = ["NTX7"]

[tool.natrix.rule_configs.MemoryExpansion]
max_frame_size = 15000

[tool.natrix.rule_configs.ArgNamingConvention]
pattern = "^_[a-z][a-z0-9_]*$"

[tool.natrix]
files = ["contracts/", "examples/"]
disabled_rules = ["NTX7"]

[tool.natrix.rule_configs.MemoryExpansion]
max_frame_size = 35000

[tool.natrix]
disabled_rules = ["NTX7"]

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This guide will help you get up and running with Natrix quickly.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: • Python (version 3.10 or higher)

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
You can install Natrix using various package managers:

pipuvpoetry 
pip install natrix

uv add natrix

poetry add natrix

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
To analyze a Vyper contract:

natrix path/to/your/contract.vy

You can also analyze multiple files at once:

natrix path/to/contracts/

docs.title: Integration with pre-commit
docs.text:
You can use Natrix with pre-commit to automatically lint your Vyper files before each commit.

  1. Add a .pre-commit-config.yaml file to your project with the following content:
repos:
- repo: https://github.com/AlbertoCentonze/natrix
rev: v0.1.7  # Use the latest version
hooks:
    - id: natrix
  1. Install the hooks:
pre-commit install

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• For advanced configuration options, see the Configuration guide
  • Explore the available Rules to understand what Natrix can check for
  • Learn more about Development if you want to contribute

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text: This section provides an overview of the Natrix API for developers creating custom rules or integrating Natrix into other tools.

docs.title: Core Architecture
docs.text: Natrix is built around several key abstractions that provide an unstable API for rule development:

docs.title: AST Processing Pipeline
docs.text:
1. Vyper Compilation: Compile .vy files to annotated AST
  2. Node Wrapping: Wrap raw AST in Node objects for easy traversal
  3. Rule Application: Apply registered rules using the visitor pattern
  4. Issue Collection: Gather and format rule violations

docs.title: Node API
docs.text: The Node class is the primary abstraction for working with Vyper AST data. It is inspired by the VyperNode class but adapted to work with json AST data.

docs.title: Basic Node Operations
docs.text:
from natrix.ast_node import Node

# Create a node from AST data
node = Node(ast_dict)

# Access node properties safely
node_type = node.get("ast_type")
function_name = node.get("name", default="unknown")

# Navigate the AST tree
parent = node.parent
children = node.children

docs.title: Node Traversal Methods
docs.text: This methods are inspired from the vyper

docs.title: get_descendants(node_type=None, filters=None, include_self=False, reverse=False)
docs.text:
Find all descendant nodes matching criteria:

# Find all function definitions
functions = root.get_descendants("FunctionDef")

# Find nodes with specific properties
assigns = root.get_descendants("Assign", filters={"target.id": "total"})

# Multiple node types
control_flow = root.get_descendants(("If", "For", "While"))

docs.title: get_children(node_type=None, filters=None, reverse=False)
docs.text:
Find immediate children only:

# Get all direct children of a specific type
statements = function_node.get_children("Expr")

docs.title: get_ancestor(node_type=None)
docs.text:
Find parent nodes:

# Find the containing function
containing_function = node.get_ancestor("FunctionDef")

# Find any ancestor matching types
container = node.get_ancestor(("FunctionDef", "For", "If"))

docs.title: Property Access with get()
docs.text:
Safely access nested properties using dot notation:

# Simple property access
line_number = node.get("lineno")

# Nested property access
target_name = node.get("target.id")
decorator_name = node.get("decorator_list.0.id")

# With default values
node_name = node.get("name", default="anonymous")

docs.title: FunctionDefNode API
docs.text:
Specialized subclass for function definitions with additional properties:

from natrix.ast_node import FunctionDefNode

# Cast or create FunctionDefNode
if node.ast_type == "FunctionDef":
    func_node = FunctionDefNode(node.node_dict, parent=node.parent)

docs.title: modifiers: List[str]
docs.text:
Get function decorators:

# Returns list like ["external", "view", "nonreentrant"]
decorators = func_node.modifiers

# Check for specific modifiers
is_external = "external" in func_node.modifiers
is_view = "view" in func_node.modifiers

docs.title: is_constructor: bool
docs.text:
Check if function is a constructor:

if func_node.is_constructor:
    # Handle constructor logic
    pass

docs.title: is_from_interface: bool
docs.text:
Check if function is from an interface definition:

if func_node.is_from_interface:
    # Skip interface functions
    return

docs.title: memory_accesses: List[MemoryAccess]
docs.text:
Get all memory read/write operations:

for access in func_node.memory_accesses:
    print(f"{access.type}: {access.var}")  # "read: balance"

docs.title: Base Rule Class
docs.text:
All rules inherit from BaseRule:

from natrix.rules.common import BaseRule, RuleRegistry
from natrix.ast_node import Node

@RuleRegistry.register
class MyRule(BaseRule):
    CODE = "NTX999"  # Unique rule identifier
    MESSAGE = "Following pattern is not allowed: {}"  # Format string for messages

    def __init__(self, custom_param="default"):
        super().__init__(
            severity="warning",  # "error", "warning", "info"
            code=self.CODE,
            message=self.MESSAGE,
        )
        self.custom_param = custom_param

docs.title: Visitor Methods
docs.text:
Implement visitor methods for specific AST node types:

class MyRule(BaseRule):
    def visit_FunctionDef(self, node):
        """Called for every function definition"""
        if self._should_flag(node):
            self.add_issue(node, "Function violates rule")

    def visit_Assign(self, node):
        """Called for every assignment"""
        pass

    def visit_Name(self, node):
        """Called for every name reference"""
        pass

docs.title: add_issue(node, *message_args)
docs.text:
Report a rule violation:

def visit_FunctionDef(self, node):
    function_name = node.get("name")
    if len(function_name) > 50:
        # Message formatting with arguments
        self.add_issue(node, function_name, len(function_name))

docs.title: Manual Registration
docs.text:
from natrix.rules.common import RuleRegistry

# Register a rule class
RuleRegistry.register(MyRule)

docs.title: Decorator Registration
docs.text:
@RuleRegistry.register  # Recommended approach
class MyRule(BaseRule):
    pass

docs.title: AST Tools API
docs.text:
Utilities for working with Vyper compilation:

from natrix.ast_tools import parse_file, vyper_compile, VyperASTVisitor

# Parse a Vyper file to AST
ast_data = parse_file("contract.vy")
root_node = Node(ast_data)

# Compile with specific format
ast_only = vyper_compile("contract.vy", "annotated_ast")
metadata = vyper_compile("contract.vy", "metadata")

docs.title: VyperASTVisitor
docs.text:
Base visitor for traversing AST nodes:

class MyVisitor(VyperASTVisitor):
    def visit_FunctionDef(self, node):
        print(f"Found function: {node.get('name')}")

    def visit_Assign(self, node):
        print(f"Assignment at line {node.get('lineno')}")

# Use the visitor
visitor = MyVisitor()
visitor.visit(root_node)

docs.title: Rule Configuration
docs.text:
Rules can accept configuration parameters:

class ConfigurableRule(BaseRule):
    def __init__(self, threshold=100, pattern="^_"):
        super().__init__()
        self.threshold = threshold
        self.pattern = re.compile(pattern)

Configuration is passed via: - TOML files: [tool.natrix.rule_configs.ConfigurableRule] - CLI arguments: --rule-config ConfigurableRule.threshold=200

docs.title: Step-by-Step Example
docs.text:
This examples shows how to apply a rule that at enforces function naming to be in snake_case.

from natrix.rules.common import BaseRule, RuleRegistry
from natrix.ast_node import FunctionDefNode
import re

@RuleRegistry.register
class FunctionNamingRule(BaseRule):
    """
    Enforces function naming conventions.

    Functions should use snake_case naming.
    """

    CODE = "NTX100"
    MESSAGE = "Function '{}' should use snake_case naming"

    def __init__(self, pattern=r"^[a-z_][a-z0-9_]*$"):
        super().__init__(
            severity="warning",
            code=self.CODE,
            message=self.MESSAGE,
        )
        self.pattern = re.compile(pattern)

    def visit_FunctionDef(self, node: FunctionDefNode):
        # Skip interface functions
        if node.is_from_interface:
            return

        function_name = node.get("name")

        # Skip special functions
        if function_name.startswith("__"):
            return

        if not self.pattern.match(function_name):
            self.add_issue(node, function_name)

docs.title: Testing Rules
docs.text:
import pytest
from natrix.ast_tools import parse_file
from natrix.ast_node import Node

def test_function_naming_rule():
    # Create test contract
    test_contract = """
@external
def badFunctionName():  # Should trigger rule
    pass

@external
def good_function_name():  # Should pass
    pass
"""

    # Parse and test
    with open("test.vy", "w") as f:
        f.write(test_contract)

    ast_data = parse_file("test.vy")
    rule = FunctionNamingRule()
    issues = rule.run(ast_data)

    assert len(issues) == 1
    assert "badFunctionName" in issues[0].message

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• Cache expensive operations using @cached_property
  • Use specific visitor methods instead of generic traversal
  • Filter early with node type and filter parameters

docs.title: Robustness
docs.text:
• Use node.get() with defaults for safe property access
  • Check node types before casting to specialized classes
  • Handle missing AST properties gracefully

docs.title: Maintainability
docs.text:
• Use descriptive rule codes and messages
  • Document rule behavior in docstrings
  • Provide configuration options for flexibility
  • Include comprehensive tests

docs.title: Development Guide
docs.text: This section provides information for developers contributing to Natrix.

docs.title: Python Version
docs.text:
Natrix requires Python 3.10 or higher. This is specified in pyproject.toml: 

requires-python = ">=3.10"

docs.title: UV Package Manager
docs.text: Natrix uses UV for dependency management and development workflows.

docs.title: Vyper Compiler
docs.text: Natrix requires a specific version of the Vyper compiler, each release of natrix is only compatible with a specific vyper compiler version.

docs.title: Creating a New Rule
docs.text: See the API Reference for detailed information on implementing new rules.

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• Issues: Report bugs or request features on GitHub Issues
  • Discussions: Ask questions on Vyperholics

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
This section documents the available rules in Natrix.

Rule Code Name Severity Description NTX1 Memory Expansion Warning Detects when a function's memory frame size is too large NTX2 Constant Naming Convention Style Ensures that constants are named in UPPER_SNAKE_CASE NTX3 Implicit Internal Style Detects when internal functions are missing the @internal decorator NTX4 Implicit View Style Detects when view functions are missing the @view decorator NTX5 Implicit Pure Style Detects when pure functions are missing the @pure decorator NTX6 Print Left Warning Detects when print statements are left in the code NTX7 Cache Storage Variable Optimization Detects when a storage variable is accessed multiple times and suggests caching NTX8 Unused Variable Warning Detects variables that are declared but never used NTX9 Implicit Export Important Detects when a module exposes all its functions using __interface__ NTX10 Unused Argument Warning Detects function arguments that are declared but never used NTX11 Argument Naming Convention Warning Ensures function arguments follow a specified naming convention

docs.title: Rule Categories
docs.text:
Rules are categorized by severity:

  • Style: Suggestions to improve code readability and consistency.
  • Warning: Potential issues that could lead to bugs or inefficiencies.
  • Optimization: Suggestions to improve gas efficiency.
  • Important: Issues that could have significant impact on security or correctness.

docs.title: Argument Naming Convention
docs.text: Property Value Rule Code NTX11 Severity Warning Configuration pattern - Regex pattern for argument naming convention (default: ^_)

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Consistent naming conventions improve code readability and maintainability. Prefixing arguments (e.g., with _) can help distinguish them from local variables or state variables at a glance.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Ensures that function arguments follow a specified naming convention. By default, arguments should start with an underscore (_).

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Consider the following Vyper code:

# Non-compliant - argument 'arg1' does not start with '_'
@external
def myFunction(arg1: uint256, _arg2: String[10]):
    pass

# Compliant
@external
def anotherFunction(_input_value: address, _another_param: bool):
    pass

In the myFunction example, arg1 would be flagged by this rule because it does not start with an underscore. _arg2 is compliant. In anotherFunction, both arguments _input_value and _another_param are compliant.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule can be customized by adjusting the naming pattern:

  • pattern (string): Regular expression pattern that argument names must match (default: ^_)

docs.title: pyproject.toml
docs.text:
[tool.natrix.rule_configs.ArgumentNaming]
pattern = "^_"  # Default: Arguments must start with underscore

docs.title: Command Line Configuration
docs.text:
# Use snake_case for arguments
natrix --rule-config ArgumentNaming.pattern="^[a-z_][a-z0-9_]*$"

docs.title: Class Instantiation
docs.text:
# Require camelCase for arguments
ArgumentNamingRule(pattern="^[a-z][a-zA-Z0-9]*$")

docs.title: Constant Naming Convention
docs.text: Property Value Rule Code NTX2 Severity Style

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Using UPPER_SNAKE_CASE for constants is a widely adopted convention that improves code readability by making it easy to distinguish constants from other variables.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Ensures that constants are named in UPPER_SNAKE_CASE.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Consider the following Vyper code:

# Non-compliant - constant 'threshold_value' is not in UPPER_SNAKE_CASE
threshold_value: constant(uint256) = 1000

# Compliant
MAX_VALUE: constant(uint256) = 1000
TOTAL_SUPPLY: constant(uint256) = 100000

In this example, threshold_value would be flagged by the rule. MAX_VALUE and TOTAL_SUPPLY are compliant.

docs.title: Implicit Export
docs.text: Property Value Rule Code NTX9 Severity Important

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Explicitly listing exported functions improves code clarity and reduces the risk of unintentionally exposing internal functions. It makes the contract's public interface clear and well-defined.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Detects when a module exposes all its functions using the __interface__ expression without explicitly listing them.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Consider you have a module erc20.vy:

# erc20


@external
def transfer():
    # ... does something ...
    pass

@external
def mint():
    # ... does something else ...
    pass

And you import it in another contract:

# my_token.vy
import erc20

# Non-compliant - exposes all functions from MyHelperFunctions, including potentially _internal_logic
exports: (erc20.__interface__) # this also exposes the function to mint, which might not always be intended

# my_token.vy
import erc20

# Compliant - explicitly lists the functions to be exposed
exports: (erc20.transfer, erc20.mint) # you can clearly tell that you are exposing a function to mint

docs.title: Implicit Internal
docs.text: Property Value Rule Code NTX3 Severity Style

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Since Vyper 0.4.0 functions without a visibility modifier will be marked as internal. Explicitly marking functions as @internal improves code clarity.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Detects when internal functions are missing the @internal decorator.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# Non-compliant - function is internal but not marked with @internal
def helper_function(x: uint256) -> uint256:
    return x * 2

# Compliant
@internal
def _helper_function(x: uint256) -> uint256:
    return x * 2

The helper_function would be flagged by this rule because it's not explicitly marked as @internal. The _helper_function is compliant.

docs.title: Implicit Pure
docs.text: Property Value Rule Code NTX5 Severity Style

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Similar to the implicit view rule. Using the @pure decorator for functions that don't access state is not enforced by the compiler, but helps querying the function off-chain and helps preventing the introduction of side effects in the function.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Detects when functions that don't access state at all are missing the @pure decorator.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# Non-compliant - function doesn't access state but isn't marked as @pure
@external
def calculate_sum(_a: uint256, _b: uint256) -> uint256:
    return _a + _b

# Compliant
@pure
@external
def calculate_sum_pure(_a: uint256, _b: uint256) -> uint256:
    return _a + _b

The first calculate_sum function would be flagged by this rule because it doesn't access state at all but is not marked as @pure.

docs.title: Implicit View
docs.text: Property Value Rule Code NTX4 Severity Style

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The compiler does not enforce functions that only read the state to be marked as @view. However omitting the modifier on a getter that is intended to be called off-chain forces you to execute an onchain transaction to query its value.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Detects when functions that only read state (but don't modify it) are missing the @view decorator.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# Storage variable
user_balance: public(uint256)

# Non-compliant - function reads state but isn't marked as @view
@external
def get_balance() -> uint256:
    return self.user_balance

# Compliant
@view
@external
def get_balance_view() -> uint256:
    return self.user_balance

The first get_balance function would be flagged by this rule because it reads contract state (self.user_balance) but is not marked as @view.

docs.title: Memory Expansion
docs.text: Property Value Rule Code NTX1 Severity Warning Configuration max_frame_size - Maximum allowed frame size in bytes (default: 20,000)

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Vyper can only pass arguments by value. When a DynArray is passed as an argument in an external function, the amount of memory allocated will be the upper bound of the array size, and not its actual size, which can lead to unexpected memory expansion (and crazy gas costs) if arrays (even small) with large bounds are passed.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Detects when a function's memory frame size is too large (by default, over 20,000 bytes).

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# Non-compliant - large array passed by value can cause memory expansion issues
@external
def process_large_array(_data: DynArray[uint256, 100_000_000]) -> uint256:
    _sum: uint256 = 0
    for i: uint256 in range(1000):
        _sum += _data[i]
    return _sum

The process_large_array function can be flagged by this rule if the memory frame size exceeds the threshold. To avoid this issue, consider passing arrays with smaller bounds until vyper implements runtime allocation for arrays.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule can be customized by adjusting the maximum frame size threshold:

  • max_frame_size (integer): Maximum allowed frame size in bytes (default: 20,000)

docs.title: pyproject.toml
docs.text:
[tool.natrix.rule_configs.MemoryExpansion]
max_frame_size = 25000

docs.title: Command Line Configuration
docs.text:
# Override threshold for a single run
natrix --rule-config MemoryExpansion.max_frame_size=25000

docs.title: Class Instantiation
docs.text:
# Custom threshold of 10,000 bytes
MemoryExpansionRule(max_frame_size=10_000)

docs.title: Print Left
docs.text: Property Value Rule Code NTX6 Severity Warning

docs.title: Background
docs.text: print statements are typically used for debugging and should be removed from production code. They can increase gas costs and don't serve any functional purpose in a deployed contract.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Detects when print statements are left in the code.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
@external
def debug_function():
    # Non-compliant - print statement left in the code
    print("Debug info")

@external
def production_function():
    # This function is compliant as it doesn't contain print statements
    pass

The debug_function would be flagged by this rule because it contains a print statement.

docs.title: Cache Storage Variable
docs.text:
Property Value Rule Code NTX7 Severity Optimization

Experimental Rule

This rule is currently experimental and not enabled by default. It is available in the codebase for testing purposes but is not yet production-ready.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Reading from storage is one of the most gas-expensive operations in Vyper (and Ethereum in general). Caching storage variables in memory when they are accessed multiple times can significantly reduce gas costs.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Detects when a storage variable is accessed multiple times in a function and suggests caching it to save gas.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# Storage variable
user_balance: public(uint256)

# Non-compliant - accessing storage variable multiple times
@external
def process_balance() -> uint256:
    if self.user_balance > 100:
        return self.user_balance - 100
    else:
        return self.user_balance

# Compliant - caching storage variable
@external
def process_balance_cached() -> uint256:
    balance_cache: uint256 = self.user_balance
    if balance_cache > 100:
        return balance_cache - 100
    else:
        return balance_cache

The process_balance function would be flagged by this rule because it accesses self.user_balance multiple times. The process_balance_cached function is compliant because it caches the storage variable.

docs.title: Unused Argument
docs.text: Property Value Rule Code NTX10 Severity Warning

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Unused arguments can indicate mistakes in the code (forgotten logic) or unnecessary parameters that increase complexity and gas costs.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Detects function arguments that are declared but never used within the function.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# Non-compliant - argument '_unused' is never used in the function
@external
def process(_value: uint256, _unused: address) -> uint256:
    return _value * 2

# Compliant - all arguments are used
@external
def process_fixed(_value: uint256, _modifier: uint256) -> uint256:
    return _value * _modifier

The process function would be flagged by this rule because the _unused argument is declared but never used within the function.

docs.title: Unused Variable
docs.text: Property Value Rule Code NTX8 Severity Warning

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Unused variables can indicate mistakes in the code (forgotten logic) or unnecessary code that increases complexity and gas costs.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Detects variables that are declared but never used within a function.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
@external
def process_data(_value: uint256) -> uint256:
    # Non-compliant - variable declared but never used
    temp: uint256 = _value * 2

    return _value

@external
def process_data_fixed(_value: uint256) -> uint256:
    # Compliant - all declared variables are used
    temp: uint256 = _value * 2

    return temp

The process_data function would be flagged by this rule because the temp variable is declared but never used.

docs.title: Intentionally Unused Variables in For Loops
docs.text:
When you need to iterate a specific number of times but don't actually use the loop variable, you can use the underscore (_) convention to indicate that the variable is intentionally unused. This prevents the linter from reporting it as an unused variable.

@external
def repeat_operation(count: uint256):
    # Non-compliant - 'i' is declared but never used
    for i: uint256 in range(count):
        # Some operation that doesn't use 'i'
        pass

@external
def repeat_operation_fixed(count: uint256):
    # Compliant - using '_' indicates intentionally unused variable
    for _: uint256 in range(count):
        # Some operation that doesn't use the loop variable
        pass

By naming the loop variable _, you signal to both the linter and other developers that the variable is intentionally unused, preventing false positive warnings.
